content_blocks.content: When we use fork(), we create a Child process with a completely isolated Virtual Memory space. The Parent and Child can no longer share variables. If the Child computes a cryptographic hash, how does it send that string back to the Parent? They cannot use RAM directly. We must establish a channel through the OS Kernel that transcends the process boundaries. The oldest, most fundamental form of Inter-Process Communication (IPC) in UNIX is the Anonymous Pipe. It is a unidirectional (one-way) data tunnel managed entirely in the Kernel's memory.

content_blocks.signature: #include <unistd.h> int pipefd[2]; if (pipe(pipefd) == -1) { perror("pipe"); } // pipefd[0] is the READ end. // pipefd[1] is the WRITE end.
content_blocks.description: The POSIX system call to create a unidirectional data channel in Kernel RAM.

parameters.name: pipefd
parameters.description: An array of exactly two integers. The Kernel will populate these with two newly minted File Descriptors.

returns.description: Returns 0 on success, or -1 on failure.

content_blocks.complexity: O(1) Kernel resource allocation. Read/Write speeds are bounded only by memory bandwidth.
content_blocks.exceptions: If the writer writes to a pipe where all read ends are closed, the OS violently sends a SIGPIPE signal to the writer, usually terminating it.

content_blocks.content: A Pipe acts exactly like a FIFO queue in Kernel space (usually 64KB capacity). To establish cross-process routing, the Parent must call pipe() before calling fork(). Because fork() duplicates the File Descriptor table, the Child inherits access to the exact same pipe. Because a pipe is unidirectional, data must only flow one way. If the Child is sending data to the Parent, the Child MUST close pipefd[0] (the read end), and the Parent MUST close pipefd[1] (the write end). If you forget to close the unused ends, the Kernel cannot correctly detect EOF (End of File), causing the reader to block infinitely in a deadlock.

content_blocks.caption: An architecture diagram showing a Parent and Child process writing and reading from opposing ends of a unidirectional Pipe.
content_blocks.alt_text: Visual showing Child Process writing data to FD 1 (Write End), routing through a Kernel Buffer, and being read by the Parent Process on FD 0 (Read End).

fill='var(--success-border)'/></marker></defs></svg>

content_blocks.header: Streaming Data from Child to Parent via Anonymous Pipe (Assessment Check)

questions.question: Why is it architecturally critical for the Parent process to close its copy of the pipe's Write End (`pipefd[1]`) if it only intends to read from the pipe?

options.text: To prevent the Parent from accidentally writing to its own read buffer.
options.explanation: While true, the larger architectural issue involves EOF blocking.

options.text: The OS Kernel will not signal an End-Of-File (EOF) to the reading process until EVERY open Write End referencing that pipe is completely closed. If the Parent keeps its own Write End open, its `read()` call will block infinitely in a deadlock waiting for data that will never arrive.
options.explanation: Unclosed pipe ends are the number one cause of deadlocks in multi-process Unix topologies.

options.text: It frees up exactly 4MB of Kernel memory.
options.explanation: Closing the FD removes a table entry, but doesn't immediately free the pipe buffer until all descriptors close.
Waitpid operates on process states, not file descriptors.

header_hub.title: Named Pipes (FIFOs) Persisting in the Filesystem

content_blocks.content: Anonymous pipes (pipe()) are flawless, but they possess a fatal limitation: they can only be used by processes that share a direct ancestry (a Parent and its forked Child). What if you write a Python script that needs to stream data instantly into a separately compiled, independently running C binary? Since they don't share a Parent, they don't share a File Descriptor table. To solve this, we leverage the UNIX "Everything is a File" philosophy. We create a Named Pipe (FIFO). It is a pipe that possesses an actual filename in the filesystem (e.g., /tmp/my_pipe), allowing completely unrelated, sovereign processes to route data to each other seamlessly.

content_blocks.signature: #include <sys/stat.h> if (mkfifo("/tmp/my_fifo", 0666) == -1) { ... } int fd = open("/tmp/my_fifo", O_RDONLY);
content_blocks.description: The POSIX system call to create a Named Pipe (FIFO) in the filesystem.

parameters.name: pathname
parameters.description: The path where the FIFO file will visually exist in the filesystem.

parameters.name: mode
parameters.description: Standard file permissions (e.g., 0666), restricted by the process umask.

returns.description: Returns 0 on success, or -1 on error (e.g., EEXIST if it already exists).

content_blocks.complexity: Opening a FIFO blocks until the other party opens it.
content_blocks.exceptions: Despite having a filename, data written to a FIFO NEVER touches the physical hard drive.

content_blocks.content: When you create a FIFO via mkfifo, it appears when you type ls -l. However, the file size is always 0 bytes. The data lives strictly in a 64KB RAM buffer inside the Kernel. The most jarring mechanical quirk of a FIFO is its synchronization lock. If Process A executes open("/tmp/fifo", O_WRONLY), the Kernel puts Process A to sleep immediately. The open() system call physically will not return until Process B executes open("/tmp/fifo", O_RDONLY). The Kernel refuses to let a writer open the pipe until a reader is present to catch the data, and vice versa. This forces two independent binaries to tightly synchronize their boot sequences.
Analyze the following broken snippet. Find the vulnerability before expanding the solution below.

items.question: Reveal Post-Mortem Analysis
items.answer: The engineer placed the `open()` call before the database initialization. Because a FIFO demands a peer, the `open(..., O_RDONLY)` call blocks infinitely. The daemon will completely freeze on that exact line of code until some external application opens the FIFO for writing. The database will never be initialized. Fix: Open the FIFO with the `O_NONBLOCK` flag (which allows `open` to return instantly even if no writer is present), OR initialize all internal states/databases *before* blocking on the pipe.

questions.question: What is the true physical storage medium for data pushed through a Named Pipe (FIFO) via the filesystem?

options.text: The physical Hard Drive (SSD/HDD), which is why FIFOs are slower than Anonymous Pipes.

It is exactly identical to an Anonymous Pipe. The VFS filename is merely a router alias. The actual payload data exclusively traverses an isolated RAM buffer inside the OS Kernel and NEVER touches the physical disk.
options.explanation: This guarantees that disk I/O latency does not throttle IPC communications.

options.text: The shared L1 Cache of the CPU.
options.explanation: Cache is managed by the hardware; pipes use Kernel allocated RAM buffers.
While it can be created there, the data doesn't hit the VFS blocks.

header_hub.title: POSIX Message Queues for Structured Datagrams

content_blocks.content: Pipes (both Anonymous and Named) share a crippling architectural flaw: they are unstructured Byte Streams. If Process A writes 10 bytes, and then 20 bytes, Process B might execute read() and instantly consume all 30 bytes combined into a single string. It is the programmer's job to manually parse boundaries. Furthermore, pipes cannot prioritize data; everything is strictly First-In, First-Out. If you are building a real-time trading engine, and a "Cancel Order" message arrives behind 5,000 "Buy" messages, the Cancel message must wait. We need a structure that preserves explicit message boundaries and honors message priority. We achieve this with POSIX Message Queues.

content_blocks.signature: #include <mqueue.h> mqd_t mq = mq_open("/my_queue", O_CREAT | O_RDWR, 0666, NULL); char msg[] = "CRITICAL_ALERT"; mq_send(mq, msg, sizeof(msg), 10); // Priority 10 mq_receive(mq, buffer, msg_len, &prio);
content_blocks.description: The robust POSIX interface for prioritized, boundary-preserving datagram transmission.

parameters.name: mq_open
parameters.description: Creates or opens a queue. Note that POSIX queue names MUST begin with a slash (/) and cannot contain other slashes.

parameters.name: mq_send
parameters.description: Pushes a distinct message onto the queue. The 4th argument is an integer Priority.

parameters.name: mq_receive
parameters.description: Pops the oldest, highest-priority message off the queue perfectly preserving its original byte count.

returns.description: A descriptor handle similar to a File Descriptor.

content_blocks.complexity: Slightly heavier overhead than Pipes due to tracking message boundaries and priority sorting.
content_blocks.exceptions: Requires linking with the Real-Time library (-lrt compiler flag).

content_blocks.title: Staff Engineer Invariant Laws
content_blocks.content: • The System V Banishment: UNIX has an older, highly deprecated IPC standard called System V (SysV). This involves functions like msgget(), msgsnd(), and msgrcv(). You are forbidden from writing new code using System V IPC. It has no standard file descriptor integration, relies on catastrophic global integer keys, and behaves wildly across OS flavors. You MUST exclusively utilize the modern mq_* POSIX Message Queue API.

Unlike a Pipe which appends bytes to the tail of a buffer, mq_send() inserts a distinct object into a sorted Kernel list. If the queue contains 100 messages with Priority 0, and you inject a new message with Priority 10, the OS Kernel instantly inserts that message at the very front of the queue. The very next mq_receive() call is guaranteed to extract the Priority 10 message. Furthermore, mq_receive() fails with EMSGSIZE if the destination buffer is not large enough to hold the maximum possible message size defined by the queue, completely eliminating partial-read buffer vulnerabilities.

questions.question: What is the primary architectural advantage of a POSIX Message Queue over a Named Pipe (FIFO)?

options.text: Message queues exist on the hard drive, providing persistence across reboots.
options.explanation: Both reside predominantly in RAM and vanish if unlinked.

options.text: Pipes are unstructured byte streams, susceptible to partial reads and accidental concatenation. Message Queues preserve strict message boundaries (datagrams) and allow high-priority messages to jump to the front of the queue.
options.explanation: This structured routing guarantees that complex application states can be passed safely without manual Application-Layer boundary parsing.

options.text: Message Queues don't require the Kernel to allocate memory.
Message Queues can transmit arbitrary C pointers between isolated processes.
options.explanation: Transmitting raw pointers across isolated processes is always fatal; memory maps differ.
